task: I’m helping a friend figure out whether her paycheck looks right after a weird schedule change, and I want to make sure I’m not giving bad advice. Can you help me walk through what usually needs to be checked on a pay stub and what would make it worth asking payroll to recalculate it?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the safest way is to check the stub against the **schedule, rate, and rules** first.

## What to check on the pay stub
Look at:

- **Pay period dates**
- **Hours worked**
  - regular
  - overtime
  - double time, if applicable
- **Hourly rate or salary rate**
- **Any shift differentials / premiums**
- **Bonuses, commissions, or adjustments**
- **Deductions**
  - taxes
  - benefits
  - garnishments
  - anything unusual
- **Net pay vs gross pay**
- **Year-to-date totals** if something looks off over time

## What to compare it against
Use:
- the actual schedule
- time clock records
- manager-approved changes
- policy on overtime, breaks, and shift differentials
- any written message about the schedule change

## Things that often cause mistakes
- a shift moved but the hours weren’t updated
- overtime not counted correctly
- wrong rate after a role change
- missed premium pay for nights/weekends/holidays
- unpaid training, meeting time, or required prep time
- deductions that changed without clear explanation

## When it’s worth asking for a recalculation
It’s worth raising if:
- the hours don’t match the schedule or time records
- the rate is wrong
- overtime or premiums are missing
- the pay period includes a schedule change that wasn’t reflected
- the numbers don’t add up from gross to net
- the same issue happened more than once

## What to bring to payroll
Have:
- the pay stub
- the schedule
- time records
- the written approval or message about the change
- the exact dates and hours you think are wrong

## Simple way to frame the question
> “Can you help me compare the pay stub to the schedule and time records? I want to make sure the hours, rate, and any premiums were applied correctly.”

If you want, I can give you a **quick pay-stub checklist** or help you compare a specific stub line by line.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right checklist. I’d add one thing: make sure the **schedule change itself** was actually approved in writing, not just mentioned in passing. That’s where people get burned.

What I’d verify, in order:
- pay period dates
- regular vs overtime hours
- any premium/shift diff/holiday rate
- whether the rate changed mid-period
- PTO or unpaid time coded correctly
- deductions that are new or unusually high
- whether the gross pay math actually matches the hours and rate

Worth pushing back on payroll if:
- hours on the stub don’t match the schedule/timeclock
- overtime is short by even a little
- the wrong rate got used after a shift change or role change
- a premium shift wasn’t paid
- the same pattern shows up more than once
- the stub has a correction or adjustment line and nobody explained it

I’ve had to do this kind of audit before. Last time, the issue wasn’t obvious on the face of the stub — it was the schedule record and a supervisor change that didn’t carry through. So yeah, don’t just glance at net pay. Compare source records.

If you want to make it easy on payroll, ask something plain like:
> Can you confirm the hours, rate, and any premiums used for this check? The schedule changed that week and I want to make sure it was applied correctly.

That’s usually enough to get an actual review instead of a shrug.
